items.q: How do you say 'I am 15 years old'?
items.why: Spanish uses tener (to have) for age; students translate the English 'I am' literally with ser or estar.

items.correct: To be fit / in good shape.
items.misconception: To be standing in a queue.
items.random_wrong: To be filling in a form.
items.why: Students translate 'forma' as a paper form, missing the common idiom meaning physical fitness.

items.q: Which is correct for 'She is a doctor'?
items.why: Spanish normally drops the indefinite article with professions after ser, unlike English 'a doctor'.

items.q: Which sentence correctly says 'I get on well with my sister'?
items.correct: Me llevo bien con mi hermana.
items.misconception: Me llevo bueno con mi hermana.
items.random_wrong: Yo llevo bien con mi hermana.
items.why: It's Me llevo bien con mi hermana.. “Me llevo bueno con mi hermana.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is the correct use of ser/estar: 'I am tired'?
items.why: It's Estoy cansado.. “Soy cansado.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many forms does the Spanish adjective 'simpático' have (masc sing, masc plur, fem sing, fem plur)?
items.why: The real figure is 4 forms. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
